coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of glucose based on gold nanoparticles coupled with silver nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Yan
Wu, Yiting
Di, Junwei
coredata.dc:description: We have coupled gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to assemble a plasmonic sensing platform for colorimetric detection of glucose. In this system, small AuNPs (~4nm) can act as glucose oxidase (GOD) mimic enzyme to catalytically oxidize glucose in the presence of oxygen, producing hydrogen peroxide, which dissolves AgNPs to lead the color changes. Glucose can be detected not only by naked eyes (from yellow to red) but also by spectrophotometer in the concentration range of 5–70μM, with detection limit of 3μM. More importantly, we found that l-cysteine added in the system can markedly improve the selectivity for the detection of glucose. The proposed method was used to application for the detection of glucose in human serum with satisfactory results. This system is simple and low cost without using any enzymes and organic chromogenic agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose
Colorimetric method
Gold nanoparticles
Silver nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AuNPs-AgNPs solution (A), TEM image of the AuNPs-AgNPs (B), the EDX pattern of AuNPs-AgNPs (C), the EDX elemental mapping images of Ag-K (D), Ag-L (E), Au-L (F), and Au-M (G).

The UV–vis absorption spectra of AgNPs before and after addition of different H2O2 concentration in phosphate buffer (pH7.0) solution (A), and the plot of the peak absorbance decrease versus H2O2 concentration (3.2–32μM) (B). UV–vis absorption spectra of the AuNPs-AgNPs system after addition of glucose in the presence of O2 or N2 atmosphere (C), and results of the glucose detection with GOD-AgNPs and AuNPs-AgNPs systems (D). Error bars represent the standard error derived from three independent measurements.

Effect of the pH solution (A), volume of the AuNPs added in the system (B), and incubation times and temperatures (C) on the response (ΔA) of the colorimetric detection of 30μM glucose in the AuNPs-AgNPs system.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of the AuNPs-AgNPs system before and after addition of different concentration of glucose and the calibration curve of the colorimetric detection (inset). (B) Visual color change at different concentration of glucose. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

Plot illustrating the ΔA of the absorption peak for colometric detection of 20μM glucose with the same concentration of potential substances except for fructose of 4μM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
Schematic illustration of the reactions for colorimetric detection of glucose based on Au NPs coupled with Ag NPs.
Detection of glucose in human serum.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All samples were diluted 200-fold for detection.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of glucose based on gold nanoparticles coupled with silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have coupled gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to assemble a plasmonic sensing platform for colorimetric detection of glucose. In this system, small AuNPs (~4nm) can act as glucose oxidase (GOD) mimic enzyme to catalytically oxidize glucose in the presence of oxygen, producing hydrogen peroxide, which dissolves AgNPs to lead the color changes. Glucose can be detected not only by naked eyes (from yellow to red) but also by spectrophotometer in the concentration range of 5–70μM, with detection limit of 3μM. More importantly, we found that l-cysteine added in the system can markedly improve the selectivity for the detection of glucose. The proposed method was used to application for the detection of glucose in human serum with satisfactory results. This system is simple and low cost without using any enzymes and organic chromogenic agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is a very important molecule in metabolic homeostasis, energy source and human bodily functioning [1,2]. Especially, the blood glucose is one of the important indicators of the health of human, especially for patients with diabetes. Therefore, glucose detections have attracted much attention in the research and application because diabetes is a major health problem in the world [3–5]. In many technologies, colorimetric method has attracted great attention due to its simplicity, low-cost instruments, portability, and practicality. The typical colorimetric detection of serum glucose relies on the use of glucose oxidase (GOD) [4,6]. GOD catalyzes the oxidation of glucose by oxygen to produce gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The product of H2O2 is measured by oxidation of chromogenic agents in the presence of peroxidase. However, natural enzymes have some serious shortcomings [7,8], such as high expense, insufficient stability, and easy inhibition of the catalytic activity.

In recent years, a wide variety of nanomaterials have been developed in the application for the colorimetric detection of glucose because of their particular characterization [4,9,10]. For examples, some of nanomaterials, such as Fe3O4 [8], ZnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles [11,12], nitrogen-doped grapheme quantum dots [13], C60-carboxyfullerenes [14], graphite-like carbon nitrides [15], and CuS nanoparticles [16], exhibit intrinsic peroxidase like activity similar to that found in HRP and apply to colorimetric detection of glucose with help of GOD. Moreover, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were also used as chromogenic agent to sensing H2O2 [17,18].

Among the nanomaterials, AuNPs and AgNPs have been greatly applied in optical sensing for detection of glucose [19–22]. The main methods for colorimetric detection of glucose includes: (1) Formation of metal nanoparticles reduced directly by glucose in an alkaline medium and high temperature (70-80°C) [23,24]; (2) Aggregation/anti-aggregation of metal nanoparticles by concanavalin A (Con A, a well-known glucose binding protein) [25,26]; (3) Color change of AuNPs based on the decreasing pH value by the production of gluconic acid in the glucose and GOD reaction [27]. (4) Measurement of the H2O2 product in the GOD-catalyzed system by the aggregation of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) [28,29], etching of gold or silver nanoparticles [30–34], and enlargement of Au NPs [6,35].

Recently, it was reported that small Au NPs (~3.5nm) were possessed intrinsic GOD-like activity, which also catalyzes aerobic oxidation of glucose to generate gluconic acid and H2O2 [36–38]. On the basis of this mimic GOD, Fan et al. developed colorimetric method for detection of glucose using AuNPs coupled with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and organic chromogenic agent [39]. Liu et al. also investigated this glucose oxidation reaction based on nanoceria but extremely toxic substance of KCN was used to dissolve the AuNPs in the process [40]. In this work, we report a simple colorimetric method for detection of glucose based on small Au NPs coupled with silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs). We also demonstrated that the proposed method can be used to detection of serum glucose in clinic samples. The sensing mechanism is exhibit in Scheme 1 . In the presence of oxygen, Au NPs catalyze the oxidation of glucose to form gluconic acid and H2O2. Then the Ag NPs added in the solution are etched by the product of H2O2, which induces a change of the localized surfaceplasmon resonance (LSPR) band strength [31,33]. The original color of the AuNPs-AgNPs solution was yellow, which mainly come from the color of AgNPs. The yellow of AgNPs was gradually fading with etching of the AgNPs. When AgNPs were completely dissolved by the H2O2 which was produced by the oxidation of glucose, the red color of AuNPs was observed. Therefore, this method provides a colorimetric detection of glucose using the couple of Au NPs and Ag NPs without any enzymes and organic chromogenic agents.

Silver nitrates (AgNO3) was purchased from shanghai reagent factory (China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O), citric acid (C6H8O7), disodium hydrogen phosphate(Na2HPO4), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), glucose, fructose, l-cysteine, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid(DA), paracetamol (PA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glycine, were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals were of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) obtained by a Milli-Q system.

An Agilent Cary-60 spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. TEM images were taken using a Tecnai G20 (FEI, U.S.A.) transmission electron microscope operated at 200kV. Ultrasonic cleaner from Kunshan ultrasonic instrument company was used for dispersing solutions.

The Au NPs solution was prepared using NaBH4 as reducing agent [36] and sodium citrate as a stabilizer. Briefly, 0.5mL of 10mM HAuCl4 and 0.5mL of 10mM sodium citrate solution were added into the 19mL water. Then 0.5mL of 0.1mM NaBH4 (new preparation) were injected into the solution under stirring at room temperature. The solution color changed immediately from colorless to orange red. After the solution was stable, 0.4mL of 0.1mM l-cysteine was added into the solution. The resulted Au NP solution was stored in 4°C. The LSPR band of AuNPs displayed one peak at ~508nm and TEM image exhibited that these AuNPs were spherical with average diameter of 4.0±1.2nm (Fig. S1).

The silver nanoparticles were also synthesized with NaBH4 and sodium citrate [41]. 0.5mL of 10mM AgNO3 and 0.5mL of 40mM sodium citrate solution were added into the 19mL water. Then 0.5mL of 0.1mM sodium borohydride (new preparation) was injected to the solution under the magnetic stirring in ice water bath. The color of the solution was changed at once from colorless to yellow. After the solution was stable, 0.4mL of 0.1mM l-cysteine was added into the solution. The synthesized yellow colloidal silver was stored at 4°C. The LSPR peak of the AgNPs colloid was located at ~393nm and the average size of spherical AgNPs was measured as 10.8±3.5nm from the TEM image (Fig. S1).

To a series of 5mL test tubes, 200μL of AuNPs solution was added in the mixed solution of 400μL of 0.05M phosphate buffer solution (pH7.0) and 1.40mL water saturated with oxygen. Then 300μL of glucose with different concentration was added and incubated for 5min. Next, 700μL of AgNPs solution was added and incubated for another 60min. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded against water in the room temperature.

It has demonstrated that small AuNPs exhibit an intrinsic GOD mimetic activity [36,37] and citric acid does not inhibit their catalytic activity [38]. In our experiments, both of Au NPs and Ag NPs were fabricated by reducing metal ions with NaBH4 in the presence of sodium citrate. l-cysteine was finally added for improvement of selectivity. Fig. 1A shows LSPR bands of AuNPs-AgNPs located at ~393 and ~508nm, which was close to the peak of AuNPs or AgNPs separately. Fig. 1B exhibited the TEM image of AuNPs-AgNPs. For further investigation of the composition of the structure, elemental mapping were performed with the TEM-EDX technique. Fig. 1C reflected the EDX pattern of AuNPs-AgNPs and Fig. 1D–G showed the elemental mapping of Ag and Au images. It suggested that the surface of nanoparticles was formed Au-Ag alloy.

We first examined the performance of AgNPs as the H2O2 sensing platform. The amount of decrease in LSPR absorption intensity of AgNPs corresponding to the H2O2 concentration could be directly recorded using the spectrophotometry. Fig. 2A shows the change of optical density before and after addition of H2O2 in the aqueous AgNPs solution. After addition of H2O2, the LSPR band of AgNPs solution decreased, demonstrating the AgNPs was dissolved by H2O2 [33,34]. This was confirmed by the TEM images in Fig. S2. A linear calibration curve was obtained by plotting peak absorbance decrease (ΔA=A0 −A) versus H2O2 concentration in the range of 3.2–32μM (R2 =0.9983), with detection limit of 1.4μM (3σ/k) for the system.

Next, we try to couple AuNPs and AgNPs for colorimetric detection of glucose. As shown in Fig. 2C, the LSPR band of Ag NPs decreased after addition of glucose in the presence of oxygen. However, the absorption spectrum of the solution was almost not changes after addition of glucose when the solution was aerated by N2 gas. The results confirmed that the oxidation of glucose to produce H2O2 requires the existence of O2. This is consistent with the mechanism of the oxidation of glucose catalyzed by AuNPs as a GOD mimic enzyme.

In order to evaluate the catalysis ability of AuNPs for glucose oxidation, we use GOD catalyze the oxidation of glucose to generate H2O2 which etch the AgNPs to induce a colorimetric change. In experiments, the excess of GOD (100U/mL) and AuNPs (200μL) was added. The results of LSPR peak density decreases for detection of 30μM glucose were compared in Fig. 2D. The ΔA value resulting from AuNPs-AgNPs was very close to that of GOD-AgNPs system, indicating that the proposed AuNPs can be used to replace GOD in the colorimetric detection of glucose.

The solution pH is an important factor for the catalytic activity of the AuNPs. Fig. 3A displays the GOD-like activity of AuNPs at the different pH value (5–10). It is found that a maximum changes in the absorbance decrease (ΔA) was obtained in the pH range of 6–8. It was suggested that the glucose oxidation reaction is an Eley-Rideal mechanism where an adsorbed glucose molecule reacts with an oxygen molecule in aqueous solution [37]. The neutral medium may favour the reaction. In addition, the AuNPs was not stable in strong acidity/alkalinity solution. Therefore, pH7.0 of phosphate buffer solution was chosen in the next experiments.

The effect of AuNPs concentration is investigated by fixing Ag NPs concentration at the absorbance density of 0.7 and glucose concentration of 30μM. The volume of AuNPs is varied from 100 to 500μL. Fig. 3B shows the effect of AuNPs concentration on the response of ΔA for the LSPR peak of AgNPs. The ΔA value increased when the volume of AuNPs was less than 200μL. This phenomenon is similar to enzyme-catalytic system as the concentration of the enzyme is low compared with the substrate. However, as further increasing the concentration of AuNPs, the ΔA value decreased. This may be attributed to the interference between AgNPs and AuNPs at high concentration. Fig. S3 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of AgNPs with addition of different concentration of AuNPs. With increasing concentration of AuNPs, the band of AgNPs became low in intensity, red-shift in peak wavelength, and wide in shape. Therefore, 200μL of AuNPs was selected in the experimental sensing procedures.

Fig. 3C shows the time-dependent ΔA of LSPR peak after addition of AgNPs at various incubation temperatures (15-35°C) in the detection of glucose. It was found that the reaction time was shortened after the rise of temperature. The necessary reaction time decreased from ~80min at 15°C to ~40min at 35°C. Moreover, the final stable ΔA value gradually decreased with increasing incubation temperature, which was attributed to the escape of H2O2 at high temperature. Therefore, too high incubation temperature should be avoided. In our experiment, the incubation was carried out at about 25°C for 60min.

To evaluate the analytical performance of the sensing method for detection of glucose under the optimized conditions, the UV–vis spectra in the presence of different concentration of glucose was recorded. With the increasing of the concentration of glucose, the LSPR peak of AgNPs was gradually decreased (Fig. 4A). The ΔA was linearly relation to the glucose concentration in the range of 5–70μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.997. The detection limit of this method was estimated (3σ) about 3μM. The comparison of analytical performance with other colorimetric detection of glucose was listed in Table S1. It can be seen that the proposed method based on AuNPs-AgNPs system shows good sensitivity for the detection of glucose. Moreover, a proper reducement of the concentration of AuNPs-AgNPs could further approve the LOD, but decrease linear range of detection. In addition, it was observed that the peak of AuNPs seemed decrease with decreasing the intensity of AgNPs band after addition of glucose in Fig. 4A. This was attributed to the influence of AgNPs band, which was confirmed in Fig. S4.

In the experiments, a significant color of the AuNPs-AgNPs solution changed from yellow to orange and then to red was observed (Fig. 4B), indicating the concentration of the glucose can also be detected by naked eyes. The yellow color comes from the AgNPs, and the red color comes from AuNPs. This result was also demonstrated by the SEM images of AuNPS-AgNPs system after addition of different glucose concentration. As shown in Fig. S5A, the large AgNPs still existed in the TEM image of orange solution when 40μM of glucose was added in the system. However, only small AuNPs were observed in the TEM images of red solution as 70μM of glucose was added (Fig. S5B). All those results were consistent with the mechanism shown in Scheme 1.

To examine the selectivity of the AuNPs-AgNPs system for glucose detection, the same concentration of possible interferences of typical substances on the determination of glucose (20μM) were investigated. In the absence of l-cysteine in the system, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+ and glycine did not interfere the glucose detection, but ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), paracetamol (PA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and fructose caused serious interference (Fig. S6). This phenomenon was consistent with the previous report [40]. In order to improve the selectivity, l-cysteine, a small biomolecule, is used to modify AuNPs because it can self-assemble on the surface of gold through thiol group (SH) [42–44]. For BSA, it has a high affinity with the surface of noble metal nanoparticles, which result in the adsorption of BSA on nanoparticles [45,46]. It is likely that AuNPs self-assembled with l-cysteine is stable enough from adsorption of protein which can inhibit the activity. This was demonstrated in the experiment (Fig. S7). The improvement of selectivity for small species may be attributed to the zwitterionic form of cysteine. However, the detail mechanism is not very clear at present time. Fig. S8 exhibits the colorimetric response for glucose in the absence and presence of l-cysteine. There had no marked difference between both of them, indicating that l-cysteine adsorption does not inhibit the reactions involved in this system. Fig. 5 shows the ΔA of LSPR peak of AgNPs after incubation with glucose and potential interference substance. It was found that 1-fold of K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, glycine, AA, UA, PA, BSA and 0.2-fold of fructose did not interfere the glucose detection. It was reported that fructose was 1.7±1.6mg in per 100mL blood when glucose was 86±3.3mg [47], indicating that fructose was in low concentrations under physiological conditions compared with glucose. Therefore it is potential application in the detection of glucose in practical analysis.

To test the applicability and reliability of AuNPs-AgNPs system, we detected the concentration of glucose in human serum. The human serum samples were obtained from Suda hospital. In the analysis, the sample was diluted 200 times and measured with standard curve method. Table 1 provides the analytical results obtained from this sensing platform and the hospital method. There has no marked difference between both methods and satisfactory recoveries, indicating that this colorimetric method can be potentially applied in the detection of glucose in human serum samples.

In summary, we coupled AgNPs and AuNPs to assemble AuNPs-AgNPs nanoconjugate and investigated its sensing platform for colorimetric detection of glucose. The AuNPs catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to produce H2O2, which then dissolved AgNPs to induce change of LSPR band of AgNPs. This coupled system provides a colorimetric assay of glucose. The small AuNPs can rival nature enzymes because of their easy preparation, good stability and low cost, while the AgNPs can be used sensing probe with high sensitivity avoiding toxic organic chromogenic agent. The proposed system integrated with advantage of AuNPs and AgNPs shows great promise for glucose detection with simple, robust, and cost-effective. As a proof of concept, the conjugated nanomaterials show great potential applications for the development of biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21475092) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2016.09.023.
